text:
Clouds are a sink and flux space that continually modify the atmospheric aerosol composition through heterogeneous processes occurring within the cloud reactive environment. Much remains to be learned regarding the exact chemical makeup of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) and the mechanisms and extent to which AOM is affected by cloud processes. Two supercooled cloud water samples were collected on June 23, 2008, at Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.), in the Steamboat Springs Mountains of Colorado. We identified approximately 3000 unique molecular formulas using a reversed-phase solid-phase extraction technique and analysis with ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry.

Species with molecular weights up to 700 Da were observed and could be classified into CHO, CHNO, CHOS, and CHNOS types. An average O/C ratio of about 0.6 was derived, which is higher than that of the water-soluble organic carbon typically reported in aerosol studies. The more oxidized composition of cloud organic nitrates, as deduced from the O/C ratio, is consistent with the aqueous-phase processes occurring in clouds.

The AOM is dominated by biogenic secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and residential wood burning. We identified 60% of the CHO formulas present in cloud water aerosol as being also present in laboratory SOA produced from the ozonolysis of monoterpenes (such as α-pinene and d-limonene) that are also biogenic. The CHNO class was found to be the largest and most diverse including compounds produced during NOx-dominated photochemical oxidation and those characteristic of wood smoke sources. The nighttime sample had a greater diversity of multi-nitrogen containing formulas that were attributed to nitrate radical chemistry at night. Reduced sulfur containing sulfur compounds were also found in the cloud water sample and their sources require additional investigation. In summary, this work has identified an unambiguous molecular signature of cloud water organic anions in the Rocky Mountains and quantifies the impacts of aqueous processes on the atmospheric environment.
